FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data from baseline through 3-year follow-up (Data Release 5.1, 2016-2022); secondary analysis of adolescents ages 10-14 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Problematic social media use (PSMU) measured via Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ); SMAQ sum score (range 6-36) assessed social media addiction symptoms

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects linear regression testing association between ACEs at Y1 and PSMU at Y3; multiple inferential tests with F-statistics and p-values reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Reported β = 1.5 for high ACEs vs low ACEs (p < 0.001); also reported β = 1.49 for high ACEs in Table 2

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using mixed-effects linear modeling with secondary ABCD data across 3-year follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Issues in Mental Health Nursing, 2025; not a preprint or deposit

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual psychological predictors (emotional/behavioral dysregulation) and reports ACEs (demographic) → non-SM outcome (PSMU) association; cross-sectional analyses present alongside longitudinal; FT-EC7 does not apply per rule (b) and (c) exclusions
notes: Strong inclusion; prospective longitudinal design with validated scales and multiple statistical tests; FT-EC7 ruled out because emotional dysregulation is an individual characteristic predictor

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD Study), with children from 11,875 enrolled cohort; 8,324 children analyzed at baseline; longitudinal 4-year follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use measured via Youth Screen Time Survey (estimated time spent on social media); also measured video games and television separately; social media as distinct exposure variable

FT_IC3.evidence: Longitudinal mixed-effects models testing association between social media use and ADHD inattention symptoms; reported β [SE], 0.03 [0.01]; P < .001 for average social media use and inattention symptoms

FT_IC4.evidence: Reported β = 0.03 [SE 0.01] for social media × time; cumulative 4-year effect β = 0.15 [SE 0.03]; P < .001

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal cohort study with annual follow-up for 4 years; analyses of associations between digital media use and ADHD symptoms

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Pediatrics Open Science, 2025; original research article, not preprint

FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports social media → ADHD inattention symptoms (SM as IV, not DV); includes individual psychological predictor (genetic predisposition via PGS-ADHD); longitudinal direction supports inclusion; FT-EC7 not applicable
notes: Strong inclusion; large sample size (N=8324), longitudinal design with genetic control; social media as independent variable predicting ADHD outcomes; robust methodology

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study; 1,940 participants aged 11-12 years at Wave 1, analyzed over three annual waves

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media addiction (SMA) scores from Cronbach α = 0.84 adapted questionnaire; time spent on social media also measured; both phone/SM-specific variables separately extractable

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression testing prospective associations between SMA and binge eating (BE); reported RR [95% CI] for cross-sectional (Wave 1 & 3) and prospective (Wave 1 → Wave 3) associations

FT_IC4.evidence: Reported RR = 2.82 [95% CI 1.61-4.93] for SMA addiction vs no symptoms predicting BE in crude model; Table 3 shows multiple RR estimates with 95% CIs

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal and cross-sectional analyses using secondary ABCD data; modified Poisson regression with latent class trajectory modeling

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Obesity (Silver Spring), 2025; original research article, not preprint or deposit

FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports SM → BE outcome (SMA as IV, not DV); includes individual psychological/clinical predictor (eating disorder diagnosis via KSADS-5); cross-sectional analyses present; FT-EC7 not applicable
notes: Strong inclusion; prospective longitudinal design with cross-sectional validation; multiple moderation analyses by sex and BMI; robust statistical models with reported effect sizes
